---
title: "Predicting Housing Prices Using Multiple Regression"
author:
  - name: "Author 1"
  - name: "Author 2"
  - name: "Author 3"
  - name: "Author 4"
format:
  html:
    theme: default
    toc: false
    embed-resources: true
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry: margin=1in
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.width = 5,
  fig.height = 3.5,
  fig.align = "center"
)

library(tidyverse)
library(GGally)
library(broom)
```

## Abstract

<!-- 1 paragraph: what you set out to learn + what you found. Write this LAST. -->


## Introduction

<!-- 2-3 sentences: What factors drive residential housing prices? -->
<!-- Can we build a regression model to predict Price from property characteristics? -->
<!-- Why does this matter? (home buyers, sellers, appraisers, etc.) -->


## Data Set

<!-- How was the data collected? Describe the dataset briefly. -->
<!-- ~1,735 observations, 16 predictors + 1 response (Price) -->
<!-- Note the Test column (0/1) — train/test split indicator -->

```{r load-data}
housing <- read.delim("housing-prices.txt", header = TRUE)

# Split using the Test column, then drop it
train <- housing %>% filter(Test == 0) %>% select(-Test)
test  <- housing %>% filter(Test == 1) %>% select(-Test)
```

<!-- Variables:
  Numeric:     Lot.Size, Age, Land.Value, Living.Area, Pct.College,
               Bedrooms, Fireplaces, Bathrooms, Rooms
  Binary:      Waterfront (0/1), New.Construct (0/1), Central.Air (0/1)
  Categorical: Fuel.Type, Heat.Type, Sewer.Type
  Response:    Price
-->

```{r summary-table}
# train %>%
#   select(Price, Living.Area, Land.Value, Age, Lot.Size, Bathrooms, Rooms) %>%
#   pivot_longer(everything()) %>%
#   group_by(name) %>%
#   summarise(Mean = mean(value), SD = sd(value),
#             Min = min(value), Max = max(value)) %>%
#   knitr::kable(digits = 0, caption = "Summary statistics for key variables")
```

```{r eda-plots, fig.cap="Exploratory plots of key predictors against Price."}
# ggplot(train, aes(x = Living.Area, y = Price)) +
#   geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
#   geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)

# ggplot(train, aes(x = factor(Waterfront), y = Price)) +
#   geom_boxplot()
```


## Analysis

<!-- Variable selection: forward/backward/both — if they agree, show only once -->
<!-- Convert categoricals to factors: Fuel.Type, Heat.Type, Sewer.Type -->
<!-- Consider Waterfront, New.Construct, Central.Air as factors too -->
<!-- Transformations: justify via residual plots, NOT by comparing R² across scales -->
<!-- If you transform Price, re-run feature selection -->
<!-- Independence = rows are independent, NOT that predictors are uncorrelated -->

```{r factor-conversion}
# train <- train %>%
#   mutate(
#     Waterfront    = factor(Waterfront),
#     New.Construct = factor(New.Construct),
#     Central.Air   = factor(Central.Air),
#     Fuel.Type     = factor(Fuel.Type),
#     Heat.Type     = factor(Heat.Type),
#     Sewer.Type    = factor(Sewer.Type)
#   )
```

```{r variable-selection}
# full_model <- lm(Price ~ ., data = train)
# step_model <- step(full_model, direction = "both", trace = 0)
# summary(step_model)
```

```{r residual-diagnostics, fig.cap="Residual diagnostic plots for the final model."}
# par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
# plot(step_model)

# Fix linearity FIRST before checking normality/homoscedasticity
# Curved residuals vs fitted = linearity violated — must address
```


## Results

<!-- Interpret key coefficients with units and REALISTIC ranges -->
<!-- e.g. "A 100 sq ft increase in Living.Area is associated with a $X increase in Price" -->
<!-- Use actual data ranges — don't interpret 1-unit if range is tiny -->
<!-- Report R², adjusted R², residual standard error -->
<!-- Statistical significance ≠ practical importance — discuss effect sizes -->
<!-- Waterfront likely has a large effect — contextualise it -->

```{r results-table}
# knitr::kable(tidy(step_model, conf.int = TRUE), digits = 3,
#              caption = "Final model coefficient estimates")
```

```{r test-performance}
# Evaluate on held-out test set
# preds <- predict(step_model, newdata = test)
# rmse  <- sqrt(mean((test$Price - preds)^2))
```


## Discussion & Conclusion

<!-- Limitations: observational data → no causal claims -->
<!-- Potential confounders not captured (neighbourhood, school district, etc.) -->
<!-- How to overcome? (more variables, spatial data, experimental design) -->
<!-- Practical utility: rough price estimates, identifying key value drivers -->
<!-- Transferable to other markets? Probably not directly — discuss why -->
<!-- Brief conclusion linking back to the original question -->


{{< pagebreak >}}

## Appendix

<!-- Optional 3rd page: references, large tables, extra figures -->
<!-- Code submitted separately as .R or .qmd -->
